content.text:
{I have set before you life and death, blessing and cursing: therefore choose life.}{Deuteronomy 30:19}
 {A matrix is a rectangular array of quantities set out by rows and columns.}{— James Joseph Sylvester}
We have now seen three ways to think about a system of linear equations:

items: As a collection of equations to be satisfied simultaneously

As a vector equation asking whether $\vect{b}$ is a linear 
 combination of certain vectors

content.text:
The third perspective introduces **matrix-vector multiplication**, 
a fundamental operation that unifies the first two viewpoints.

content.title: Matrix-Vector Product
content.content:
If $A$ is an $m \times n$ matrix with columns $\vect{a}_1, \ldots, \vect{a}_n$, 
and $\vect{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ \vdots \\ x_n \end{bmatrix}$ is a 
vector in $\R^n$, then the **product** $A\vect{x}$ is the linear 
combination of the columns of $A$ with weights from $\vect{x}$:
\[
A\vect{x} = 
\begin{bmatrix} \vect{a}_1 & \vect{a}_2 & \cdots & \vect{a}_n \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ \vdots \\ x_n \end{bmatrix}
= x_1 \vect{a}_1 + x_2 \vect{a}_2 + \cdots + x_n \vect{a}_n
\]

content.text:
The product $A\vect{x}$ is defined only when the number of columns of 
$A$ equals the number of entries in $\vect{x}$.

content.title: Computing $A\vect{x}$
Three Views of a Linear System
content.content:
Let $A$ be an $m \times n$ matrix with columns $\vect{a}_1, \ldots, \vect{a}_n$, 
let $\vect{b}$ be a vector in $\R^m$. The following statements are equivalent:

1. The matrix equation $A\vect{x} = \vect{b}$ has a solution.

2. The vector equation $x_1 \vect{a}_1 + \cdots + x_n \vect{a}_n = \vect{b}$ 
 has a solution.

3. The vector $\vect{b}$ is a linear combination of the columns of $A$.

4. The vector $\vect{b}$ is in $\spn\{\vect{a}_1, \ldots, \vect{a}_n\}$.

5. The augmented matrix $[A | \vect{b}]$ has a solution.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
This theorem is a cornerstone of linear algebra. It tells us that the 
question "Can we solve this system?" is *the same question* as 
"Is $\vect{b}$ in the span of the columns of $A$?"

content.title: Existence of Solutions
content.content:
Let $A$ be an $m \times n$ matrix. The following are equivalent:

1. For every $\vect{b}$ in $\R^m$, the equation $A\vect{x} = \vect{b}$ 
 has a solution.

2. Every $\vect{b}$ in $\R^m$ is a linear combination of the columns of $A$.

3. The columns of $A$ span $\R^m$.

4. $A$ has a pivot position in every row.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
The equivalence of (1), (2), and (3) follows from Theorem [thm:three-views]. 
For (3) $\Leftrightarrow$ (4): if some row lacks a pivot, then for some 
$\vect{b}$ the augmented matrix reduces to a row 
$[0 \cdots 0 | *]$ with $* \neq 0$, meaning no solution exists.

content.title: Algebraic Properties of $A\vect{x
content.content:
$}

If $A$ is an $m n$ matrix, $u$ and $v$ are in $ℝ^n$, 
and $c$ is a scalar, then:
\begin{enumerate}
 \item $A(u + v) = Au + Av$
 \item $A(cu) = c(Au)$
enumerate
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
These properties express the fact that matrix-vector multiplication is 
a **linear operation**. This notion of linearity will be central 
when we study linear transformations in Chapter [ch:transformations].

content.title: Using properties of $A\vect{x}$
content.problem:
Suppose $A\vect{u} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$ and 
$A\vect{v} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 3 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}$. 
Find $A(2\vect{u} - 3\vect{v})$ without knowing $A$, $\vect{u}$, or $\vect{v}$.
content.solution:
Using the properties:
$$
A(2\vect{u} - 3\vect{v}) &= A(2\vect{u}) + A(-3\vect{v}) \\
&= 2(A\vect{u}) - 3(A\vect{v}) \\
&= 2\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} - 
3\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 3 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ -5 \\ -14 \end{bmatrix}
$$

marginNotes.text:
In all your ways acknowledge him, and he 
will make straight your paths.
